past_medical_history: Eosinophilic vasculitis diagnosed 3 months ago, treated with methotrexate (discontinued 2 weeks prior to transfer due to elevated liver enzymes)
Type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed at age 52, with peripheral neuropathy (distal symmetric sensorimotor)
Prior retinal detachment of the left eye requiring surgery at age 55
Remote history of anxiety disorder in his 40s, treated briefly with counseling, no pharmacotherapy

medications.indication: Seizure-like episodes (started 3 weeks ago by referring physician)

medications.frequency: As needed up to three times daily

family_history: Father died of myocardial infarction at age 71
Mother had type 2 diabetes and dementia (onset in her 80s)
One brother (age 65) with essential tremor diagnosed at age 58
One sister (age 57) with fibromyalgia and depression

social_history.occupation: Retired civil engineer, stopped working at age 60 due to declining health
social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, approximately 2-3 units per week. Denies recent increase.
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, 15 pack-year history, quit at age 50
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone following the death of his wife 18 months ago from breast cancer. Provides regular caregiving assistance to his 88-year-old mother who lives nearby and has mild cognitive impairment. His adult son lives abroad.
social_history.exercise: Previously walked daily, now minimal due to episodes and fear of falling
social_history.psychosocial_stressors: Wife died 18 months ago after prolonged illness. Patient describes intense grief and difficulty adjusting to living alone. He has taken on caregiving responsibilities for his elderly mother, which he finds physically and emotionally exhausting. Recent diagnosis of eosinophilic vasculitis caused significant health anxiety, particularly concerns about long-term prognosis and immunosuppression during the COVID-19 pandemic. He reports feeling overwhelmed and describes a sense of losing control over his health.

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Affect is anxious, with frequent sighing. Speech is fluent without dysarthria or aphasia. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Attention: digit span 6 forward, 4 backward. Memory 3/3 registration, 3/3 recall at 5 minutes. Clock drawing test normal (10/10). No thought disorder. Patient becomes visibly distressed when discussing his episodes and repeatedly asks whether he could have cerebral vasculitis.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3 mm to 2 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Left eye with surgical changes from prior retinal detachment repair (mild visual acuity reduction OS, 20/30). Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Face symmetric at rest and with activation. Hearing intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk throughout. Tone normal, no rigidity or spasticity. Upper extremities: inconsistent 'give-way' weakness noted on left deltoid and bilateral biceps flexion testing — strength appears 4/5 initially but collapses suddenly against resistance, then returns to 5/5 on repeat testing. With encouragement and distraction, full 5/5 strength is consistently demonstrated. Lower extremities: similar inconsistent give-way pattern in bilateral hip flexors, less prominent in knee extensors. No pronator drift. No atrophy or fasciculations. Hoover sign: when testing right hip flexion against resistance, synergistic extension of the left heel into the examiner's hand is present, then absent during direct left hip flexion testing — positive Hoover sign bilaterally.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch intact in upper extremities. Reduced pinprick and vibration sense in a stocking distribution below the mid-calf bilaterally, consistent with known diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Proprioception intact at the great toes bilaterally. No dermatomal sensory loss. Tubular (non-anatomical) sensory loss to light touch reported over the entire left arm in a 'glove' distribution with a sharp cutoff at the shoulder — this does not conform to any peripheral nerve, root, or vascular territory and splits the midline exactly.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 1+ at biceps, triceps, and brachioradialis bilaterally (globally hyporeflexic). 1+ at patellae bilaterally. Ankle jerks absent bilaterally (consistent with diabetic neuropathy). Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus. No Hoffman sign.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Patient walks with an unusual lurching pattern with excessive swaying side to side but does not fall. When observed walking to the bathroom while he believes he is unobserved, gait is significantly more fluent and normal. Tandem gait performed with dramatic difficulty, arms widely abducted, but no actual falls. Romberg test: marked swaying with eyes closed but improves when distracted by conversation.
neurological_exam.additional: During the examination, two brief episodes were witnessed. Episode 1: Patient reported a pulsating sensation in his ears, then developed facial grimacing and rhythmic tremor of both lower limbs progressing to violent shaking of all extremities lasting approximately 15 seconds. He remained conversant throughout, stating 'it's happening again.' No loss of consciousness, no tongue biting, no incontinence. Oxygen saturation remained 98%, heart rate 76 bpm during the event. Episode 2: Following the shaking, patient became unresponsive for approximately 90 seconds — eyes closed, did not respond to verbal commands or sternal rub. However, when his hand was held above his face and dropped, he deflected it away from his face (hand-drop test positive for non-organic unresponsiveness). On recovery, he stated he had been fully aware throughout but could not move or speak. No post-ictal confusion. Toes developed transient peripheral cyanosis (Raynaud-like, attributed to vasculitis). Staff noted events occurred almost exclusively in the presence of observers.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent seizure-like episodes with limb shaking and periods of unresponsiveness for 4 weeks, unresponsive to levetiracetam and diazepam
patient.history_present_illness:
Mr. Harrington is a 62-year-old right-handed Caucasian man transferred from a regional hospital to the neurology service for further evaluation of recurrent paroxysmal episodes that began approximately four weeks ago. These episodes were initially attributed to possible epileptic seizures in the context of his recently diagnosed eosinophilic vasculitis, raising concerns for cerebral vasculitis-related seizures.

The episodes typically begin with a pulsating or whooshing sensation in both ears, followed by a wave of tension and aching pain that radiates down his limbs. This prodromal phase lasts 10-20 seconds before progressing to visible facial grimacing and bilateral lower extremity tremor that can escalate to violent shaking of all four limbs. The movements are irregular, waxing and waning in intensity, and each shaking episode lasts between 10 and 45 seconds. The patient reports remaining fully conscious and aware throughout the motor phase and can describe what is happening in real time. He denies any loss of consciousness, tongue biting, or urinary or bladder incontinence during any episode.

Following the motor phase, he enters a period of apparent unresponsiveness lasting one to three minutes during which he lies still with his eyes closed. He reports being fully aware during these periods but states he is unable to move or speak. Staff report that his vital signs remain entirely stable throughout, with normal oxygen saturation, heart rate, and blood pressure during and after events. He also describes palpitations associated with the episodes, though continuous telemetry at the referring hospital showed no arrhythmias during witnessed events.

Notably, his toes develop transient peripheral cyanosis during episodes, which the referring team attributed to his vasculitis. The episodes occur two to four times daily and are almost exclusively witnessed by nursing staff or family, rarely occurring when the patient is alone. The episodes have been refractory to a trial of levetiracetam (500 mg twice daily for three weeks) and low-dose diazepam (2 mg as needed), which were started empirically at the referring hospital.

The patient is significantly distressed about these episodes and expresses recurrent fears that his vasculitis has spread to his brain. He lost his wife to breast cancer eighteen months ago and has been struggling with bereavement, social isolation, and the dual burden of managing his own new diagnosis while caring for his elderly mother. He reports poor sleep, reduced appetite, and a pervasive feeling of losing control over his body since the vasculitis diagnosis.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz well-organized alpha rhythm, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Drowsiness achieved with vertex waves and theta slowing. Brief stage II sleep with K-complexes and sleep spindles.

findings.state: Wakefulness, drowsiness, and brief stage II sleep
findings.clinical_correlation: No interictal epileptiform activity identified during this 2-hour routine recording. This does not exclude epilepsy but no supportive evidence is found.

findings.morphology: Low-amplitude fast activity superimposed on normal background
findings.clinical_correlation: Likely related to benzodiazepine use (diazepam). Nonspecific finding.

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes performed. No change in background. No epileptiform discharges induced. No clinical symptoms provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response at 10-16 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal routine interictal EEG. No epileptiform discharges. Mild diffuse beta enhancement consistent with benzodiazepine use. No clinical events were captured during this recording. The normal interictal EEG does not exclude epilepsy, but provides no supportive evidence. Prolonged monitoring with video-EEG is recommended to capture habitual events and determine whether they have an electrographic correlate.
eeg.limitations: No habitual clinical events were captured during this 2-hour routine recording. A normal interictal EEG does not exclude seizure disorders, particularly frontal lobe epilepsy. Prolonged continuous video-EEG monitoring is essential for definitive characterization of the patient's episodes.
recommended_actions: Proceed with prolonged continuous video-EEG monitoring (minimum 3-5 days) to capture habitual episodes
Correlate with brain MRI findings to evaluate for structural lesion or vasculitis-related changes
Consider post-event prolactin measurement during next captured event

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: No white matter hyperintensities beyond age-appropriate changes
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: No abnormal parenchymal or leptomeningeal enhancement

findings.location: Bilateral periventricular and deep white matter, predominantly frontal

additional_observations: No suggestion of focal edema, vasculitis-related infarctions, or hemorrhage
No evidence of CNS vasculitis — no vessel wall thickening, no multifocal infarcts, no hemorrhagic lesions
No leptomeningeal enhancement to suggest meningeal involvement
Scattered periventricular white matter hyperintensities are nonspecific and consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic disease in the context of age (62), hypertension, and diabetes
Normal vascular flow voids in major intracranial arteries
MR angiography (circle of Willis): No stenosis, aneurysm, or beading pattern suggestive of vasculitis
Bilateral ethmoid sinus mucosal thickening is an incidental finding
mri.impression: Essentially normal brain MRI and MR angiography. Scattered punctate periventricular white matter hyperintensities are nonspecific and most consistent with chronic microvascular ischemic changes related to age, hypertension, and diabetes mellitus. Importantly, there is no evidence of cerebral vasculitis — no focal edema, multifocal infarcts, vessel wall thickening, hemorrhage, or abnormal enhancement. No structural epileptogenic lesion identified. The MRA shows no beading or stenosis to suggest vasculitis.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: High - consistent with age, hypertension, and diabetes
Low - no characteristic findings on parenchymal imaging or MRA; cannot be entirely excluded without conventional angiography but no supportive evidence
Very low - white matter lesions are periventricular but lack the morphology, orientation, and enhancement pattern of MS plaques

recommended_actions: Correlate with EEG findings to evaluate for seizure disorder
If cerebral vasculitis remains a clinical concern despite normal MRI/MRA, consider conventional cerebral angiography or vessel wall MRI as more sensitive techniques
The periventricular white matter changes should not be attributed to vasculitis without additional supportive evidence
Clinical correlation with rheumatology regarding systemic vasculitis activity

findings: Mild left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (borderline)
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with borderline left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria, likely related to chronic hypertension. No acute ischemic changes. No arrhythmia.
ecg.clinical_correlation: No cardiac etiology identified for episodic symptoms. Borderline LVH consistent with hypertensive heart disease. Continuous telemetry during witnessed episodes at referring hospital showed no arrhythmias.

CBC.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated eosinophil count, consistent with underlying eosinophilic vasculitis. Currently on prednisolone taper. The eosinophilia is a feature of the systemic vasculitis but does not indicate cerebral involvement.

BMP.clinical_significance: Elevated fasting glucose, reflecting poorly controlled type 2 diabetes exacerbated by corticosteroid therapy (prednisolone).

Hepatic_Panel.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, likely residual effect from recent methotrexate therapy (discontinued 2 weeks ago due to hepatotoxicity).
Mildly elevated ALT, consistent with recent methotrexate hepatotoxicity, trending down from prior values.

Inflammatory_Markers.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated ESR, may reflect ongoing low-grade systemic inflammation from eosinophilic vasculitis despite prednisolone. Does not localize to CNS.
Mildly elevated CRP consistent with systemic inflammatory process (eosinophilic vasculitis). Not specific to CNS involvement.

HbA1c.clinical_significance: Elevated HbA1c indicating suboptimally controlled diabetes, worsened by concurrent corticosteroid therapy.

Post_Event_Prolactin.clinical_significance: Normal baseline prolactin. Post-event measurement should be obtained 15-20 minutes after a captured episode for comparison. A >2x elevation above baseline suggests epileptic seizure; normal post-event prolactin supports non-epileptic event.

labs.interpretation: Comprehensive laboratory evaluation reveals several abnormalities that relate to the patient's known comorbidities but do not specifically support a CNS process. Mild peripheral eosinophilia (0.82 x10^9/L) is consistent with underlying eosinophilic vasculitis on partial treatment with prednisolone. Elevated inflammatory markers (ESR 28, CRP 8.4) reflect ongoing systemic inflammation but are nonspecific. Mildly elevated transaminases (AST 42, ALT 58) are consistent with recent methotrexate hepatotoxicity and are trending down. Elevated fasting glucose and HbA1c 7.8% reflect suboptimal diabetic control exacerbated by corticosteroids. Thyroid function is normal. Baseline prolactin is normal. The constellation of findings — peripheral eosinophilia, elevated inflammatory markers, and a known vasculitis diagnosis — may lead to concern for cerebral vasculitis, but this must be correlated with neuroimaging and EEG findings.
abnormal_values_summary: Eosinophils (absolute): 0.82 x10^9/L (ref: 0.0-0.5) - Mild eosinophilia consistent with eosinophilic vasculitis
AST: 42 U/L (ref: 10-40) - Mildly elevated, recent methotrexate use
ALT: 58 U/L (ref: 7-56) - Mildly elevated, recent methotrexate use
ESR: 28 mm/hr (ref: 0-22) - Mild elevation, systemic vasculitis
CRP: 8.4 mg/L (ref: 0-5.0) - Mild elevation, systemic vasculitis

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, K-complexes, and sleep spindles. All sleep stages recorded. No interictal epileptiform discharges during sleep.

findings.type: Clinical event captured - motor episode with tremor and shaking (Type 1)
findings.frequency: Four events captured over 4 days (durations: 15 seconds, 30 seconds, 22 seconds, 40 seconds)
findings.morphology: During all four events, the EEG showed persistent normal waking alpha rhythm with superimposed muscle and movement artifact. No ictal pattern, no rhythmic evolution, no electrodecrement, no post-ictal slowing. Video review: episodes begin with facial grimacing, then bilateral lower limb tremor progressing to violent irregular shaking of all extremities. Movements are non-rhythmic, wax and wane in intensity, and vary in semiology between events. Patient is conversant during events — in one event clearly states 'I can feel it happening' while the EEG shows normal background. No tongue biting, incontinence, or cyanosis (except transient peripheral toe discoloration noted on video).
findings.clinical_correlation: Four habitual motor events captured, all without electrographic correlate. The preserved normal alpha rhythm throughout, retained full awareness and ability to converse during events, variable and non-stereotyped motor semiology, absence of post-ictal confusion, and waxing-waning character are all strongly consistent with psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES). Bilateral motor events of this magnitude would invariably produce EEG changes if epileptic.

findings.type: Clinical event captured - period of unresponsiveness (Type 2)
findings.frequency: Three episodes captured (durations: 90 seconds, 120 seconds, 75 seconds)
findings.morphology: Normal waking EEG persisted throughout all three unresponsive episodes. No diffuse slowing, no electrodecrement, no focal or generalized rhythmic activity. Video review: patient lies motionless with eyes closed, not responding to verbal or tactile stimulation. In one event, the hand-drop test was performed — patient deflected his hand away from his face, demonstrating preserved voluntary motor control. In another, brief eyelid flutter was observed when his name was called loudly. On 'recovery,' patient states he was aware throughout but unable to move or speak.
findings.state: Wakefulness (EEG confirms cortical wakefulness during apparent unresponsiveness)
findings.clinical_correlation: Three episodes of apparent unresponsiveness with preserved normal waking EEG, demonstrating cortical wakefulness during clinical unresponsiveness. The positive hand-drop test, eyelid flutter to auditory stimulation, and patient's report of preserved awareness confirm these are dissociative episodes (psychogenic unresponsiveness), not epileptic seizures or organic encephalopathy. No post-event confusion.

findings.morphology: Low-amplitude fast activity, unchanged from initial recording

artifacts.impact: Moderate artifact during shaking episodes, but underlying cerebral activity clearly identifiable in posterior leads where persistent alpha rhythm is visible throughout all events

output.impression: Diagnostic 4-day continuous video-EEG monitoring. Seven habitual clinical events captured (four motor episodes with tremor/shaking and three episodes of unresponsiveness), all without any electrographic correlate. The EEG demonstrated persistent normal waking background during all events. Motor events showed features diagnostic of psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES): irregular non-stereotyped movements, waxing-waning intensity, retained awareness with ability to converse, no post-ictal confusion. Unresponsive episodes showed preserved cortical wakefulness during apparent unresponsiveness, consistent with dissociative episodes. No interictal epileptiform discharges identified throughout the entire 4-day recording. Post-event prolactin was obtained after a captured motor event (9.1 ng/mL at 18 minutes post-event vs baseline 8.6 ng/mL) — not elevated, supporting non-epileptic etiology.
output.limitations: None significant. Adequate number of habitual events of both types captured. Diazepam was not tapered during monitoring given the low dose and short duration of use.
recommended_actions: Communicate the diagnosis of psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) to the patient clearly and empathically
Discontinue levetiracetam, which is unnecessary and ineffective
Referral for psychotherapy, particularly CBT for PNES and grief counseling
Evaluate for comorbid depression, anxiety, and adjustment disorder related to bereavement and health anxiety
Reassure regarding absence of cerebral vasculitis (normal MRI/MRA, normal EEG)
Post-event prolactin: 9.1 ng/mL at 18 minutes post-event (baseline 8.6 ng/mL) — not elevated, consistent with non-epileptic event

output.interpretation: Entirely normal CSF analysis. No pleocytosis, no elevated protein, normal glucose ratio. Comprehensive autoimmune encephalitis panel is negative including anti-NMDA receptor, anti-LGI1, anti-CASPR2, anti-AMPA, and anti-GABA-B antibodies. Viral PCR panel is negative, excluding infectious meningoencephalitis. Critically, there is no CSF eosinophilia, which would be expected if eosinophilic vasculitis involved the CNS. Normal ACE level argues against neurosarcoidosis. These results, combined with normal brain MRI, provide strong evidence against cerebral vasculitis, autoimmune encephalitis, or infectious etiology.

Nerve_Conduction_Studies.clinical_significance: Right: DL 5.8ms, amp 2.8mV, CV 38 m/s. Left: DL 5.6ms, amp 3.0mV, CV 39 m/s. Mildly prolonged distal latencies and reduced conduction velocities bilaterally, consistent with diabetic sensorimotor polyneuropathy.
Right: DL 5.5ms, amp 4.2mV, CV 39 m/s. Left: DL 5.4ms, amp 4.5mV, CV 40 m/s. Mildly prolonged distal latencies bilaterally.
Right: amp 3.2 microV, CV 36 m/s. Left: amp 3.5 microV, CV 37 m/s. Reduced amplitudes and conduction velocities bilaterally, consistent with distal axonal sensory neuropathy.

Nerve_Conduction_Studies.reference_range: Motor: DL <4.4ms, amp >4.0mV. Sensory: amp >20 microV
Nerve_Conduction_Studies.clinical_significance: Normal bilateral median motor and sensory studies. No carpal tunnel syndrome. Length-dependent neuropathy pattern confirmed.

Needle_EMG.clinical_significance: Rare positive sharp waves bilaterally. Mildly increased MUAP duration. Early recruitment pattern. Consistent with chronic denervation/reinnervation from diabetic neuropathy.
No denervation. Normal MUAP morphology and recruitment. No evidence of myopathy or myositis.
Normal. No evidence of myopathy, myositis, or motor neuron disease.
No fibrillations or positive sharp waves. No evidence of radiculopathy.
Normal. Evaluated to exclude PERM (progressive encephalomyelitis with rigidity and myoclonus) — no continuous motor unit activity.

output.interpretation: Electrodiagnostic studies reveal a mild, symmetric, length-dependent, predominantly axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy affecting the lower extremities, consistent with diabetic peripheral neuropathy. No convincing EMG evidence of myopathy or myositis in any muscle tested. No continuous motor unit activity to suggest PERM or stiff-person spectrum disorder. Importantly, the upper extremity studies (median motor/sensory, deltoid EMG) are entirely normal, which is inconsistent with the patient's reported give-way weakness in the upper extremities and the non-anatomical sensory loss in the left arm.
abnormal_values_summary: Peroneal motor: mildly prolonged DL and reduced CV bilaterally (diabetic neuropathy)
Tibial motor: mildly prolonged DL bilaterally
Sural sensory: reduced amplitudes and CV bilaterally (axonal sensory neuropathy)
Tibialis anterior EMG: rare positive sharp waves (chronic denervation from neuropathy)

Autoimmune_Panel.clinical_significance: Positive p-ANCA/MPO is consistent with the known eosinophilic vasculitis (EGPA — eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis) diagnosis. This confirms systemic vasculitis but does NOT confirm CNS involvement. Only ~5-10% of EGPA cases develop CNS vasculitis.
Negative serum anti-NMDA receptor antibodies. Combined with negative CSF testing, autoimmune NMDAR encephalitis is excluded.

Mildly elevated anti-GAD65 antibodies. This level (12 IU/mL) is commonly associated with type 1 or latent autoimmune diabetes (LADA) rather than neurological autoimmunity. Neurological GAD65 syndromes (stiff-person syndrome, cerebellar ataxia, epilepsy) typically require levels >1000 IU/mL. This is likely a diabetes-related finding and NOT neurologically significant.

output.interpretation: Autoimmune panel reveals positive p-ANCA/MPO antibodies consistent with known EGPA diagnosis, confirming systemic vasculitis. Mildly elevated anti-GAD65 antibodies (12 IU/mL) are at a level consistent with diabetes-associated autoimmunity rather than neurological autoimmune disease (neurological GAD syndromes require levels typically >1000 IU/mL). All neural-specific antibodies (NMDA receptor, LGI1) are negative in serum. Combined with negative CSF autoimmune panel, autoimmune encephalitis is excluded. The positive ANCA confirms systemic disease but does not indicate CNS vasculitis — cerebral involvement in EGPA is rare and requires imaging confirmation.
abnormal_values_summary: p-ANCA/MPO: 42 U/mL (positive) - confirms known EGPA diagnosis
Anti-GAD65: 12 IU/mL (ref: <5) - likely diabetes-related, below neurologically significant threshold

Psychiatric_Evaluation.value: Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder (Conversion Disorder) with attacks or seizures (F44.5); Adjustment Disorder with mixed anxiety and depressed mood (F43.23); Prolonged grief disorder (F43.8, proposed criteria met)
Psychiatric_Evaluation.clinical_significance: The bereavement (wife's death 18 months ago) is the primary precipitating psychosocial stressor. The eosinophilic vasculitis diagnosis compounded health anxiety. Caregiver burden (elderly mother) is a perpetuating factor. The convergence of multiple severe stressors in a previously psychologically well-adjusted individual created the conditions for conversion disorder.

Psychiatric_Evaluation.reference_range: 0-4 none, 5-9 mild, 10-14 moderate, 15-19 moderately severe, 20-27 severe
Psychiatric_Evaluation.clinical_significance: Moderate depression, with prominent anhedonia, sleep disturbance, appetite loss, and feelings of worthlessness
Severe anxiety, predominantly health-focused (fears about vasculitis progression, COVID-19 vulnerability, fear of losing independence)

Meets criteria for prolonged grief disorder. Patient reports persistent yearning, difficulty accepting wife's death, feeling lost without her, and inability to envision a future. Grief response has intensified rather than attenuated over 18 months.
Markedly elevated health anxiety. Patient catastrophizes about his vasculitis diagnosis, frequently checks his body for new symptoms, and repeatedly asks clinicians whether his brain is affected.

output.interpretation: Comprehensive psychiatric evaluation identifies multiple significant psychosocial factors: prolonged grief disorder related to wife's death 18 months ago, moderate depression, severe health anxiety focused on the vasculitis diagnosis, and adjustment difficulties related to caregiver burden. The temporal relationship between bereavement, new serious medical diagnosis, and onset of functional neurological symptoms is consistent with the recognized biopsychosocial model of FND. The patient's fixed belief that his symptoms represent cerebral vasculitis reflects illness anxiety that serves as a barrier to accepting the functional diagnosis. Recommendations: (1) Grief-focused psychotherapy, (2) CBT for health anxiety, (3) Consider SSRI for depression and anxiety, (4) Careful communication of FND diagnosis with validation and hope, (5) Integrated neurological-psychiatric follow-up.

output.query: eosinophilic granulomatosis polyangiitis EGPA cerebral vasculitis CNS involvement seizures

results.title: Neurological manifestations of eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Churg-Strauss syndrome): a systematic review
results.summary: CNS involvement occurs in only 5-10% of EGPA cases. The most common neurological manifestation is peripheral neuropathy (mononeuritis multiplex in 50-75%), not cerebral vasculitis. CNS vasculitis in EGPA typically presents with ischemic stroke, cranial nerve palsies, or hemorrhage and is associated with characteristic MRI abnormalities (multifocal infarcts, hemorrhage, leptomeningeal enhancement). Seizures from EGPA CNS vasculitis are rare and would be expected to show structural lesions on MRI.

results.title: Psychogenic nonepileptic seizures in patients with systemic autoimmune diseases
results.summary: Patients with chronic systemic diseases, including autoimmune conditions, are at increased risk for PNES due to psychological distress, health anxiety, and the stress of chronic illness management. The diagnosis of a serious medical condition can serve as a precipitating factor for functional neurological symptoms.

results.title: Functional neurological disorder: diagnosis and management challenges in older adults
results.source: Gelauff et al., Journal of Neurology 2020;267:3244-3253
results.summary: FND in older adults is often underdiagnosed due to clinician bias toward organic explanations. Precipitating factors in older adults include bereavement, new medical diagnoses, health anxiety, and loss of independence. The diagnosis should be made based on positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, inconsistent weakness, etc.) rather than by exclusion.

results.title: Positive signs in functional neurological disorder: Hoover sign, give-way weakness, and other examination findings
results.summary: Hoover sign has sensitivity of 63% and specificity of 100% for functional weakness. Give-way (collapsing) weakness, non-anatomical sensory loss, and inconsistency between examination and observed functional activities (e.g., normal gait when not observed) are important positive diagnostic features. These should be used to make a positive diagnosis of FND, not merely to exclude organic disease.

output.summary: CNS involvement in EGPA is rare (5-10% of cases) and typically manifests as stroke, hemorrhage, or cranial neuropathy with characteristic MRI findings — not isolated seizures with a normal MRI. Patients with chronic systemic diseases are at increased risk for PNES due to health anxiety and psychological distress. FND in older adults is frequently underdiagnosed because clinicians preferentially seek organic explanations. Positive examination signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, non-anatomical sensory loss) allow a positive diagnosis of FND.

output.proposed: Starting sertraline 50 mg daily for depression and anxiety in a patient on prednisolone, metformin, lisinopril, tamsulosin, with levetiracetam and diazepam to be discontinued
interactions: Sertraline + prednisolone: Both can affect gastric mucosa; consider GI protection. Low risk for clinically significant pharmacokinetic interaction.

Sertraline + diazepam: Sertraline can inhibit CYP2C19 and CYP3A4, potentially increasing diazepam levels. Since diazepam is being tapered and discontinued, this is a time-limited concern but monitor for excess sedation during overlap.
Sertraline + metformin: No significant interaction. Sertraline may modestly improve glycemic control by reducing cortisol-related insulin resistance.
Sertraline + tamsulosin: Sertraline inhibits CYP2D6 which metabolizes tamsulosin. May increase tamsulosin levels and risk of orthostatic hypotension. Monitor blood pressure.
warnings: Monitor for hyponatremia (SIADH), particularly in an older adult on an ACE inhibitor (lisinopril). Check sodium levels at 2-4 weeks.
GI bleeding risk is increased with SSRI + corticosteroid combination. Consider gastroprotection with a PPI if both are used concurrently.
Levetiracetam discontinuation can be done relatively quickly (taper over 1-2 weeks) as it has a short half-life and low seizure risk (patient does not have epilepsy).
alternatives: Escitalopram 10 mg daily — fewer CYP interactions but similar SIADH risk
Mirtazapine 15 mg at bedtime — may help with insomnia and appetite (patient has poor appetite and sleep), fewer drug interactions, but risk of weight gain
Cognitive behavioral therapy should be first-line and may be sufficient without pharmacotherapy

findings.location: Bilateral internal carotid arteries, middle cerebral arteries, anterior cerebral arteries, vertebral arteries, basilar artery, posterior cerebral arteries

signal_characteristics.pre_contrast: Normal vessel wall thickness throughout. No mural thickening.
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: No vessel wall enhancement in any intracranial vessel. No circumferential or eccentric enhancement.

additional_observations: No evidence of intracranial vasculitis — no vessel wall thickening or enhancement
No stenosis or beading of intracranial vessels
Patent flow in all major intracranial vessels
Vessel wall MRI is a highly sensitive technique for detecting large and medium vessel vasculitis and shows no abnormalities
This result, combined with normal conventional MRI and MRA, effectively excludes large and medium vessel cerebral vasculitis
output.impression: Normal high-resolution vessel wall MRI. No evidence of intracranial vasculitis. No vessel wall thickening or enhancement. This effectively excludes large and medium vessel cerebral vasculitis as a cause of the patient's episodes. Small vessel vasculitis cannot be entirely excluded by imaging alone but is inconsistent with the normal brain parenchyma on conventional MRI (no microinfarcts, no hemorrhage).
recommended_actions: These findings, combined with normal brain MRI and normal CSF, provide strong evidence against cerebral vasculitis
The negative vessel wall MRI should be communicated to the patient as reassurance that his vasculitis has not affected his brain

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder - psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) with dissociative episodes, in the context of eosinophilic vasculitis (EGPA), bereavement, and health anxiety

differential.likelihood: Low - the patient has known eosinophilic vasculitis (EGPA), which creates a strong anchoring bias toward cerebral involvement. However, CNS involvement in EGPA is rare (5-10%), typically presents with stroke or cranial neuropathy (not isolated seizures), and requires characteristic MRI findings (multifocal infarcts, hemorrhage, enhancement) that are absent. Normal brain MRI, normal MRA, normal vessel wall MRI, normal CSF without eosinophilia, and events without EEG correlate collectively exclude this diagnosis.
differential.key_features: Would expect MRI abnormalities (infarcts, hemorrhage, enhancement), CSF eosinophilia or pleocytosis, vessel wall thickening on VW-MRI, and if seizures were vasculitis-related they would have EEG correlates.

differential.likelihood: Low - despite the clinical appearance of seizure-like activity, video-EEG captured seven habitual events (four motor and three unresponsive episodes) with no electrographic correlate. The movements are non-stereotyped, wax and wane, and the patient retains full awareness and communicates during events. Normal post-event prolactin further supports non-epileptic etiology. Levetiracetam and diazepam had no effect on event frequency.
differential.key_features: Would expect EEG correlate during bilateral motor events, stereotyped semiology, post-ictal confusion, elevated post-event prolactin, and response to AEDs.

differential.likelihood: Very low - considered given the autoimmune background. However, autoimmune antibody panels are negative in both serum and CSF (anti-NMDA receptor, anti-LGI1, anti-CASPR2, anti-AMPA, anti-GABA-B all negative). CSF is entirely normal. Brain MRI shows no encephalitic changes.
differential.key_features: Would expect positive autoimmune antibodies, CSF pleocytosis, and MRI changes (mesial temporal signal abnormality).

differential.diagnosis: Progressive encephalomyelitis with rigidity and myoclonus (PERM) or stiff-person spectrum disorder
differential.likelihood: Very low - considered given the rigidity-like episodes and anti-GAD65 positivity. However, anti-GAD65 level is only 12 IU/mL (neurological syndromes require >1000 IU/mL), EMG shows no continuous motor unit activity, and anti-amphiphysin antibodies are negative.
differential.key_features: Would expect very high anti-GAD65 titers (>1000 IU/mL), continuous motor unit activity on EMG, and progressive course with axial rigidity.

differential.likelihood: Very low - normal ECG, continuous telemetry during events showed no arrhythmias, vital signs remain stable during all episodes, and the motor manifestations are not consistent with convulsive syncope.
differential.key_features: Would expect ECG abnormalities, hemodynamic changes during events, brief loss of consciousness.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive history including detailed semiology of episodes (prodrome, motor features, awareness, post-event state), temporal relationship to vasculitis diagnosis and bereavement, response to levetiracetam and diazepam, and psychosocial context
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Episodes with retained awareness, variable non-stereotyped movements, no loss of consciousness, no tongue biting, no incontinence, refractory to AEDs, occurring preferentially when observed — all raising suspicion for non-epileptic events

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination looking for positive signs of functional neurological disorder
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive Hoover sign bilaterally, inconsistent give-way weakness, non-anatomical (tubular) sensory loss in left arm, discrepancy between observed gait and performance on formal testing, positive hand-drop test during unresponsive episode — these are positive diagnostic signs of FND

optimal_actions.action: Order routine EEG to assess for interictal epileptiform activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal interictal EEG with benzodiazepine-related beta enhancement. No epileptiform discharges. No events captured. Provides no evidence for epilepsy but is insufficient alone — prolonged monitoring needed.

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with contrast and MR angiography to evaluate for cerebral vasculitis and structural epileptogenic lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No evidence of cerebral vasculitis — no focal edema, infarcts, hemorrhage, or abnormal enhancement. Periventricular white matter changes are nonspecific (age, hypertension, diabetes). Normal MRA. These findings effectively exclude cerebral vasculitis as the cause of episodes.

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup including CBC with differential, BMP, hepatic panel, inflammatory markers, thyroid function, HbA1c, and baseline prolactin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild eosinophilia and elevated inflammatory markers reflecting systemic vasculitis (red herrings suggesting possible CNS involvement). Elevated glucose and HbA1c from steroid-exacerbated diabetes. Normal baseline prolactin for future comparison.

optimal_actions.action: Proceed with prolonged continuous video-EEG monitoring (3-5 days) to capture habitual events and determine whether they have an electrographic correlate
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Seven habitual events captured (four motor, three unresponsive), all without electrographic correlate, diagnostic of PNES and dissociative episodes. Normal post-event prolactin. No interictal epileptiform discharges.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CSF analysis to exclude cerebral vasculitis, autoimmune encephalitis, and infectious meningoencephalitis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Entirely normal CSF with no pleocytosis, no eosinophilia (critical — excludes CNS eosinophilic vasculitis involvement), negative autoimmune encephalitis panel, negative viral PCR panel

optimal_actions.action: Diagnose functional neurological disorder (PNES with dissociative episodes) based on positive clinical signs and video-EEG evidence, not by exclusion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Diagnosis based on positive features: Hoover sign, give-way weakness, non-anatomical sensory loss, inconsistent gait, hand-drop test, plus video-EEG confirmation of events without electrographic correlate

optimal_actions.action: Communicate diagnosis clearly and empathically, explaining that episodes are real neurological events but not caused by epilepsy or cerebral vasculitis, and that effective treatments exist
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient understands that FND is a recognized neurological condition, that his brain imaging and CSF confirm no vasculitis involvement, and that psychological treatment is the evidence-based approach

optimal_actions.action: Discontinue levetiracetam and taper diazepam. Refer for psychotherapy (CBT for PNES, grief counseling) and psychiatric evaluation for depression, anxiety, and prolonged grief disorder
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Cessation of unnecessary AEDs and initiation of appropriate psychological and psychiatric treatment addressing bereavement, health anxiety, and caregiver stress

critical_actions: Perform prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture habitual events and determine whether they have an electrographic correlate — this is the gold standard for differentiating epileptic seizures from PNES

Recognize and document positive clinical signs of FND on examination (Hoover sign, inconsistent give-way weakness, non-anatomical sensory loss, gait discrepancy, hand-drop test) — FND should be diagnosed positively, not by exclusion
Do not be anchored by the eosinophilic vasculitis diagnosis into assuming cerebral vasculitis explains the episodes — CNS involvement in EGPA is rare and would show MRI abnormalities
Appropriately exclude cerebral vasculitis and autoimmune encephalitis through imaging and CSF analysis before finalizing FND diagnosis, given the autoimmune background
Communicate the FND diagnosis with empathy, validating that symptoms are real, explaining the mechanism, and providing clear reassurance that brain imaging excludes vasculitis involvement
Discontinue unnecessary antiepileptic medication (levetiracetam) and taper diazepam
Address the underlying psychiatric comorbidity (bereavement, health anxiety, depression) that precipitated and maintains the functional symptoms
contraindicated_actions: Starting additional antiepileptic drugs or escalating levetiracetam dosage without first performing video-EEG to confirm the episodes are epileptic
Escalating immunosuppressive therapy (e.g., cyclophosphamide, rituximab) for presumed cerebral vasculitis without imaging or CSF evidence of CNS involvement
Telling the patient that his episodes are fake, imagined, or 'all in his head' — this is harmful and clinically incorrect
Performing invasive procedures (cerebral angiography, brain biopsy) for vasculitis workup when MRI, MRA, vessel wall MRI, and CSF are all normal
Interpreting the mildly elevated anti-GAD65 (12 IU/mL) as evidence of stiff-person spectrum disorder — this level is consistent with diabetes, not neurological autoimmunity
Attributing the periventricular white matter changes to vasculitis — these are age-appropriate microvascular ischemic changes
Dismissing the psychological factors (bereavement, health anxiety, caregiver stress) as irrelevant to the presentation
key_reasoning_points: The diagnostic puzzle centers on a patient with confirmed systemic eosinophilic vasculitis (EGPA) who develops seizure-like episodes, creating a strong anchoring bias toward cerebral vasculitis as the cause. The agent must resist this anchoring and evaluate the episodes on their own merits.

The positive examination signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, non-anatomical sensory loss, gait discrepancy, hand-drop test) allow a positive diagnosis of FND — these are not signs of exclusion but have high specificity for functional neurological disorder.

The peripheral eosinophilia and elevated inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) are red herrings in the context of the neurological presentation — they reflect systemic vasculitis activity but do not indicate CNS involvement. CSF eosinophilia would be expected if vasculitis affected the CNS.

The mildly elevated anti-GAD65 (12 IU/mL) is a significant red herring — it could suggest PERM or stiff-person spectrum disorder, but neurological GAD65 syndromes require levels typically >1000 IU/mL. This low-titer positivity is consistent with type 2 diabetes.

Normal brain MRI, normal MRA, normal vessel wall MRI, and normal CSF collectively provide very strong evidence against cerebral vasculitis. The periventricular white matter hyperintensities are consistent with age, hypertension, and diabetes — not vasculitis.

Video-EEG monitoring capturing seven events without electrographic correlate is diagnostic of PNES. The bilateral violent motor events would invariably produce EEG changes if they were epileptic seizures.

The psychosocial context is crucial: recent bereavement, new serious medical diagnosis, health anxiety, caregiver burden, and social isolation create a perfect convergence of precipitating and perpetuating factors for FND.

The transient peripheral cyanosis (Raynaud-like phenomenon) during episodes is a red herring attributed to the vasculitis, but it is actually a peripheral vascular phenomenon that can occur with hyperventilation and autonomic arousal during PNES.
Events occurring almost exclusively when observers are present is a well-recognized feature of PNES, not malingering — FND symptoms are involuntary and not deliberately produced.
The response to levetiracetam and diazepam (no improvement) should prompt reconsideration of the seizure diagnosis rather than escalation of AED therapy.

FND in older men is less recognized than in young women, creating a diagnostic blind spot. The male sex and older age may delay the FND diagnosis as clinicians search more aggressively for organic explanations.

red_herrings.data_point: Eosinophilic vasculitis (EGPA) diagnosis with peripheral eosinophilia and elevated inflammatory markers
red_herrings.intended_effect: Anchor clinicians toward cerebral vasculitis as cause of seizure-like episodes, prompting unnecessary immunosuppressive escalation
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: EGPA rarely involves the CNS (5-10%). Peripheral eosinophilia and elevated ESR/CRP reflect systemic disease activity but do not indicate CNS involvement. Normal brain MRI, MRA, vessel wall MRI, and CSF without eosinophilia exclude cerebral vasculitis.

red_herrings.data_point: Periventricular white matter hyperintensities on brain MRI
red_herrings.intended_effect: Could be misinterpreted as demyelination from vasculitis or MS, supporting an organic explanation for the episodes
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Scattered punctate periventricular WMH in a 62-year-old with hypertension and diabetes are consistent with chronic microvascular ischemic disease. They lack the morphology, size, and enhancement pattern of vasculitis-related or MS lesions.

red_herrings.intended_effect: May suggest stiff-person spectrum disorder or PERM, given the rigidity-like episodes and limb stiffness
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Anti-GAD65 at 12 IU/mL is far below the threshold for neurological autoimmune syndromes (>1000 IU/mL). This low titer is commonly seen in type 2 diabetes and reflects pancreatic autoimmunity, not neurological disease.

red_herrings.intended_effect: Confirms active autoimmune disease and could reinforce the assumption that an autoimmune process explains the neurological episodes
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Positive ANCA confirms the known systemic EGPA diagnosis but says nothing about cerebral involvement. CNS vasculitis requires imaging and CSF confirmation, both of which are normal.

red_herrings.data_point: Transient peripheral cyanosis of toes during episodes
red_herrings.intended_effect: Suggests vascular involvement during episodes, potentially supporting vasculitis-related mechanism
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Peripheral (Raynaud-like) cyanosis can occur with autonomic arousal, hyperventilation, and vasospasm during psychogenic episodes. In a patient with known vasculitis, it is a peripheral vascular phenomenon, not evidence of CNS vasculitis.

red_herrings.data_point: Brother with essential tremor and paternal aunt with epilepsy
red_herrings.intended_effect: Brother's tremor may suggest a familial movement disorder; aunt's epilepsy may anchor toward genetic seizure predisposition
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Essential tremor in a sibling and childhood-onset epilepsy in a distant relative are common findings in the general population and have no bearing on this patient's functional neurological symptoms.

red_herrings.data_point: Diabetic peripheral neuropathy with abnormal EMG/NCS in lower extremities
red_herrings.intended_effect: Could suggest a more diffuse neuromuscular process or vasculitis-related neuropathy, complicating the diagnostic picture
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: The EMG/NCS findings show a classic symmetric length-dependent axonal sensorimotor neuropathy consistent with diabetes, not mononeuritis multiplex (which would be expected in EGPA neuropathy). Upper extremity studies are normal, contradicting the patient's reported upper extremity weakness.

metadata.condition_name: Functional neurological disorder - Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) with dissociative episodes
metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle due to coexisting eosinophilic vasculitis (EGPA) creating strong anchoring bias toward cerebral vasculitis, multiple inflammatory lab abnormalities serving as red herrings, mildly elevated anti-GAD65 suggesting stiff-person spectrum disorder, periventricular white matter changes on MRI, and atypical demographics (older male) for FND
metadata.sub_domain: Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures with systemic autoimmune anchoring
metadata.estimated_complexity: Diagnostic puzzle - systemic autoimmune disease masking functional neurological disorder, multiple red herrings from vasculitis workup, atypical demographics
